Fast Track Your Job Application with a Personalized Approach

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Bonnie Dilber Bonnie Dilber is an Influencer
2mo
  • Report this post

Back in January 2022, I was hiring a technical sourcer, and I posted a way to fast track your app: Take a role we're currently hiring for, source one candidate for it, and send over how you found them and why they're a fit. Do you want to know how many people took me up on that? *drumroll please* One. One person actually took the time to source a single candidate and DM me. They were awesome, became a finalist for the role, and we're still connected and in touch to this day even though they ended up moving in another direction. I also got a lot of people saying, "I don't work for free" or "I'm not jumping through hoops for a job." And that's fine, everyone has their own boundaries. But it was a role that had hundreds of apps and this one person was able to stand out from the crowd by spending 20 minutes showing how they would do the work. And personally, I'd rather spend 20 minutes going all out for a role I want than 20 minutes clicking easy apply apps for jobs I don't. Now most companies don't post a "fast track" like this, but you CAN probably get yourself fast-tracked by trying something like this anyway. What might this look like? - if you're a recruiter and find a role at a company you'd love to work at, pick a role, find some folks in a creative way, and message a recruiting manager sharing your approach. - if you're in marketing, create a quick mock campaign for one of their products (a few sample social posts, a headline, a target audience) and send it with your thinking. - if you're in sales, record a 2–3 minute Loom walking through how you'd pitch their product to a specific customer segment - if you're in customer success, pick one of their customers and outline a simple onboarding or retention plan - if you're in product, write a short doc with 2–3 product improvements you'd suggest and why - if you're in design, redesign one small part of their site or product and explain your decisions - if you're in content, write a sample blog post, LinkedIn post, or email newsletter in their voice - if you're early in your career, create a deck sharing how you'd ramp up in the first 30 days - and if they're clearly AI-pilled, build a cool workflow with AI that would be applicable to them, and record a video walking through it And while personalizing to a company is best, creating something generic and applicable to many companies my go further if you're looking for something scalable. Sure it takes more effort. And no, it won't overcome being unqualified (though I have seen many HMs take a chance on a stretch candidate who shows this kind of drive and hustle!) But the folks who operate this way are creating more opportunities and access for themselves.

109 42 Comments
Like Comment
Laura Stubbins, MFA 2mo
  • Report this comment

Great suggestions! I'll check out Loom today 😀

Like
Reply
1 Reaction
Joselyn N. 2mo
  • Report this comment

100% this. I completely changed tack on my job search a couple of months ago and focused on building a portfolio of things that demonstrate my skillset (agentic apps, various Zaps, a portfolio website itself)... I also apply to *maybe* 2-3 jobs/week now and spend the rest of my time building and learning. The results have been dramatic: I went from an almost a 0 response rate to 4 interviews in the last 2 weeks. I knew I had to find a way to stand out in a sea of AI-generated resumes and it seems like this new approach is working! Plus I'm having way more fun.

Like
Reply
9 Reactions 10 Reactions
Alex Shinkarovsky 2mo
  • Report this comment

Do you feel as job apps continue to be saturated, such work challenges will proliferate more? I love this one.

Like
Reply
2 Reactions 3 Reactions
Ashleigh Bergh Duggan 2mo
  • Report this comment

I think there's a balance between "I don't work for free" and "let me show you how I work". Just like it is impossible for a candidate to truly understand what it is like to work for any company, with the existing team, under a manager, the same goes for the company trying to assess and learn how the candidate would/could do the job. It's not the norm, but I know at least 3 people from different industries who got interviews because they showed their "how".

Like
Reply
4 Reactions 5 Reactions
Sam Struan 2mo
  • Report this comment

This is exactly how I see the hiring process evolving – employers may want some form of verifiable proof (e.g. Loom video) where the person can be seen doing the actual work. A somewhat related example – I once received a DM pitch to fix my website. Unlike other pitches, this person sent me a mock up before and after example (proof they could do the job) Below is the Google Doc example they sent me. They took my site and mocked up a front page that was WAY better than my own. Immediately had my attention and it stood out against any other DM message. Note: I wasn't soliciting for site updates either, this was a cold pitch. We didn't end up working together for a full site overview but I did adapt my page based on their pitch and I sent them nearly $200 as a thank you and because I did use their idea in the end. Like you said – not saying do lots of free work but I do recommend demonstrating actual ability to do the job. Nice examples in the post Bonnie. Thank you.

Reply
3 Reactions 4 Reactions
Ave Leslie, Ph.D. 2mo
  • Report this comment

I agree, every job searcher has a small number of companies/roles that would be the dream job. This level of extra effort doesn't become the baseline for every single application. I've also seen versions of this idea where the work task is intentionally stale, so there's no risk of candidates seeing it as exploitation. (For instance, asking candidates to source a role from last year instead of a current one.) But some candidates find live challenges exciting, so it can go either way.

Like
Reply
1 Reaction
Andrea Lyles, (M.A.) 2mo
  • Report this comment

I created a portfolio with mock docks and workflows. I put it out on LinkedIn and at the top of my resume and on the coverletters I have sent out. So far it has not landed. But I like the way you think. I even took on a 30 trial with Articulate 360 to see if I could learn it quickly as alot of roles I had been looking at used that platform for their Content and Learning platforms. I love learning new things and dig in to find all the ways to make it easier to skill up quickly. I know the right role is out there. It is just a matter of finding it and sticking out enough to land the job.

Reply
1 Reaction
Savannah Hill 2mo
  • Report this comment

Love this. Showcasing your skills and spending some effort to do so, in my opinion, is not "working for free". It is showcasing how you'd be the best for the role! As roles get more skills-forward and more competitive, this will help job seekers stand out! It's not one size fits all but I love this advice. Thanks for sharing!

Reply
1 Reaction
Jordan Campbell, MBA 2mo
  • Report this comment

I think this is a good idea with boundaries, of course! Taking a few extra minutes to reach out and/or be creative can be the difference between a new role and sitting unemployed for months, I'd rather do 10 strong applications a week than spam 50 a day with no real strategy. It's just a matter of getting pass the mindset of " never working for free" and reframing it as, " building your portfolio and skill bank".

Like
Reply
1 Reaction
Ryan Stewart 2mo
  • Report this comment

When someone shows how they think and approach the work, it cuts through a lot of the noise that resumes and applications create. It gives a much clearer signal than keywords ever will. That said, it also highlights a gap on the company side. If candidates feel like they have to create their own “fast track,” it usually means the hiring process isn’t doing a great job of identifying that signal on its own.

More Relevant Posts

  • Martin Dubreuil
    1mo
    • Report this post

    Stop competing with 500 applicants for the same opportunity. By the time a job reaches LinkedIn, Indeed, or major job boards… you’re already late. Now you’re one resume in a giant pile. The smarter strategy? Reach companies before they advertise. Thousands of small businesses need: marketers, sales people, designers, fractional executives, assistants, developers, operations help, AI support, and freelancers. Most simply never post publicly. That’s why proactive outreach is becoming more powerful than passive applying. Your next opportunity may literally start with one message. alpa.mindrasolutions.com

    1
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Muhammed Umar
    2mo Edited
    • Report this post

    I heard from a recruiter 5 years ago it took 500 emails to make one hire. Today it takes 5,000. And AI made it WORSE. Not better. WORSE. Your startup can't compete. And you don't even know it yet. Every recruiter on earth now has AI tools blasting thousands of "personalized" messages daily. So every engineer on earth now gets thousands of "personalized" messages daily. When everyone has the same AI tools, nobody has an advantage. You just made the noise 10x louder. While you're posting "We're hiring!" on LinkedIn with a rocket emoji, the engineer you want is deleting 50 AI-crafted messages from companies that all sound identical. Your job post isn't getting SEEN. It's buried under Google, Stripe, and 47 funded startups who all messaged that engineer before breakfast. After building 53+ startups, here's the brutal truth: YOU ARE INVISIBLE. - 400 applications. 380 garbage. 15 bots. 4 decent. 1 good. They ghosted. - Your recruiter sent 5,000 AI-generated emails. Got 3 replies. One was "unsubscribe." - Your "personalized" outreach reads exactly like everyone else's "personalized" outreach. AI handles 80% of recruiting now. But the 20% that actually closes a hire - trust - relationships - reputation AI made that HARDER by drowning it in noise. And your hiring strategy is STILL "post and pray." HERE'S WHY YOU KEEP LOSING CANDIDATES: You're competing on salary against companies with unlimited budgets. You'll NEVER win that fight. The founders who actually hire great people? They stopped competing on money years ago. They compete on TRUST. You can find a senior engineer at any company right now. Finding them isn't the problem. Getting them to CARE is. Cold message from a startup they've never heard of? Delete. Their former coworker saying "these guys are building something insane, you need to talk to them"? Response in 5 minutes. Same engineer. Same opportunity. Completely different outcome. The best hires I've made in 8 years ALL came from: - Engineers who worked with us and came back - Referrals from the team - Relationships built over years, not job boards 5,000 emails to make one hire means cold outreach is DEAD. Most founders just haven't buried it yet. Stop hiring like it's 2015. Start building a network people actually want to join. Nobody responds to strangers anymore. They respond to people they trust. Become that person or lose every candidate to someone who already is.

    17 2 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Jackie Van Over MLS
    1mo Edited
    • Report this post

    The biggest lie in modern hiring is that applying online is a strategy. It’s not. It’s a volume game. Most companies are overwhelmed with applications. Recruiters are overloaded. ATS systems filter by keywords before a human even looks at a resume. Meanwhile, candidates are told to “keep applying” as if effort alone guarantees visibility. It doesn’t. LinkedIn was built to scale professional networking and recruiting operations, not to create a perfect meritocracy. And somewhere along the way, hiring became performance marketing: Algorithms. Engagement. Keywords. Personal branding. Application volume. That doesn’t mean talented people disappeared. It means signal-to-noise collapsed. The strongest candidates I know are not sitting around mass applying all day. They are: Building relationships. Having direct conversations. Creating niche expertise. Positioning themselves clearly. Solving business problems before they’re hired. The future of hiring is moving back toward trust, reputation, and strategic connection, not endless application funnels. If you’re job searching right now, stop believing visibility equals value. It doesn’t. The goal is not to be seen by everyone. The goal is to be undeniable to the right people.

    1 Comment
    Like Comment

  • Anubhav Jain
    1mo
    • Report this post

    How to find marketing roles that aren't on job boards? 99% marketers don't know this hack👇🏼 It’s called BOOLEAN Search. It pulls up hiring posts straight from Hiring Managers.   Here's how to do it in 3 simple steps👇🏼   -------   STEP-1/ Go to the Linkedin Search Bar   -------   STEP-2/ Type a search query using this template:    “keyword” AND “role” AND “location”   Replace:   ↳ “role” with your target job title ↳ “location” with target location ↳ “keyword” with one of these: - Join - Hiring - Seeking - Looking - Opening - Recruiting   Examples:   1.“Recruiting” AND “Brand Manager” AND “Austin” 2.“Seeking” AND “Product Marketing Manager” AND “Chicago” 3.“Hiring” AND “Marketing Manager” AND “New York”   -------   STEP-3/ Apply Filters   ↳ Select 'Posts' ↳ Set ‘Date Posted’ to Last Week or Last Month. That’s it! You’ll now see live hiring posts —ones that never make it to job boards. Here’s a link with the 3 steps executed: https://lnkd.in/gksVuSBc → Modify and Bookmark it → Visit every-day to find hidden jobs. → Apply quickly and send a DM to the hiring manager. “Hi [Name], saw your post about [Role]. I’m a [your title] with [x years experience] in [function] where I achieved [major career achievement]. I believe I’d be a strong fit. Looking forward to connecting” ---------- Pro Tips:   1. “Remote” can be used as “Location” criteria e.g. “Looking” and “Social Media Manager” and “Remote”   2. “Role” can be replaced with “Key Skills” e.g. “demand generation” instead of  “demand generation manager”   The best roles are often not listed. They're hidden in plain sight. P.S. If you want help executing your boolean search or the messaging the hiring manager, DM me "BOOLEAN".

  • Read Startup

    96 followers

    1mo
    • Report this post

    5 Linkwdin job search hacks that get you hired faster Credits to Reno Perry follow for more impactful content. -------------- Here's the original post The best 6-figure offers don't go to the best candidates. They go to the ones who follow a system, not luck. Applying to jobs feels like shouting into a void. Recruiters get 250+ applications for every role. I’ve seen many get 1000+ Your resume has a few seconds to make an impact. But there's a better way to stand out. After helping hundreds land amazing roles,  I've noticed a pattern: The most successful job seekers aren't  using the same LinkedIn as everyone else. They're using these 5 essentials that most people miss: 1️⃣ Your profile is your sales page, not your resume Headline: Role + industry Focus + your Impact About: Tell us who you are, what you do, why it matters Featured: Showcase wins with real evidence 2️⃣ One good comment can outperform ten average posts Comment on executives' content (they notice) Add genuine insight to show your credibility. Ask a thoughtful question to build a relationship 3️⃣ Consistency beats viral content every time LinkedIn rewards weekly posters with 5x more  profile views and 7x more connections. Your formula: Start with a valuable insight Add a quick personal example End with a simple call-to-action 4️⃣ The DM template that gets 50%+ response rates: "Hey [Name], I saw your post on [topic].  Really resonated with your take.  I've worked on something similar,  curious how your team is approaching [X] right now?" 5️⃣ The hidden "Open to Work" hack Set your status to "Recruiters Only" -  you'll get the algorithm boost  without broadcasting to your network. The job market has SERIOUSLY changed. Most recruiters find talent on LinkedIn, not job boards. Most still apply the old way and remain invisible. While others wait for luck to strike, you need to be creating your own opportunities. These 5 essentials are your system for standing out in 2026. --------- Follow Ai Sales Edge for more

    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Austin Belcak
    1mo
    • Report this post

    I applied to 300+ jobs. Got rejected from all of them. I decided to try a new approach. The result? Interviews at Google, Twitter, and Uber + an offer from Microsoft with a $70k+ raise. Here's the 9 step system I used: 1. Understand Who Companies Really Hire I used to think the most qualified candidate got the job. That's not true. Companies don't hire the most qualified candidate. They hire the person they believe will deliver the most value. My new goal? Be that person. 2. Go Deep On Value-Aligned Companies To illustrate value, I had to narrow my focus. I stopped applying to random jobs online. Instead, I created a system for scoring opportunities. When I found a fit? I aimed to learn everything I could about goals, challenges, and initiatives. 3. Find People Who Can Refer You In I knew I needed a referral. An internal advocate would help me skip the line and maximize my value. So I used LinkedIn to find people who could influence my ability to get hired. - Potential hiring managers - Skip level managers - Peers I aimed for 15+ at each company. 4. Start Making Contact I didn't ask for a referral up front. I made my initial outreach about the other person: - I engaged with their content - Recognized career transitions - Asked for advice and followed up with results After a few touch points, I asked for a call. 5. Identify Opportunities For Value I used these calls to perform discovery. I'd ask my contacts about: - Their journeys - The biggest challenge their team is facing - The six month goals for their team I'd look for any opportunity where I could illustrate tangible value. Then I’d do a deep dive into that opportunity. 6. Research, Research, Research With my potential opportunity in hand, I'd start researching. My favorite tactics were: - Listening to earnings calls - Interviews / keynotes with executives - Product + customer reviews - Surveying customers / prospects - Performing competitive analyses Then I'd prepare my pitch. 7. Crafting A Pitch Deck I packaged my pitch in a short slide deck: - I introduced the problem / opportunity. - I backed it up with industry data. - I shared 2-3 ideas or solutions with an execution plan. Then I sent the deck to my contact and asked for feedback. After some back and forth, I’d go for the referral. 8. Examples of Ideas For Google, I created a personalized pitch strategy for a potential client. For Twitter, I shared 5 ideas for monetizing their existing audience. For Microsoft, I surveyed SMBs and turned the feedback into a strategy to help Microsoft Ads reach more SMBs. Anything works here as long as it’s valuable to the company and allows you to convey that value on your terms. That was my story. I've spent the past 10 years refining that system with 1,000+ clients. Now it helps them land amazing roles 2x faster with a $44k raise, right now, in this market. Stuck in your search? Let's talk, free call here 👉 https://lnkd.in/d5Wpe5Wp

    214 131 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Sadaf Tahir
    2mo
    • Report this post

    🚨 STOP clicking “Easy Apply” if you actually want the job. I know it feels productive. One click. No friction. Instant application. But in most cases? “Easy Apply” is giving you the illusion of progress — not actual momentum. Here’s why: 1) LinkedIn benefits when you click it Companies pay for that traffic. So the platform is naturally incentivized to keep you using the feature — even if it doesn’t lead to better outcomes for job seekers. 2) You get buried in a flood of unqualified applicants “Easy Apply” makes it ridiculously easy for anyone to apply, qualified or not. That creates a mess: - Hiring teams get overwhelmed - Strong candidates get lost in the noise - Applicant numbers look way scarier than they really are. - In many cases, only 5–15% of applicants are actually qualified. So no — you’re not always competing with hundreds of serious candidates. You’re often competing in a pile of noise. 3) Online applications have become the slow lane With AI and mass applying, job boards are more crowded than ever. Which means the easiest way to apply is often the easiest way to be ignored. The people getting interviews aren’t just applying faster. They’re applying smarter: ✅ Tailoring their resume ✅ Reaching out to recruiters or hiring managers ✅ Using referrals ✅ Building real human connections ✅ Following up strategically Bottom line: Don’t confuse convenience with strategy. Before you hit “Easy Apply” again, ask yourself: Am I actually applying for this job… or just hoping to get lucky? 👇 What’s worked better for you: Easy Apply, direct outreach, or referrals? Drop your experience in the comments. ♻️ And repost this to help someone job searching right now.

    17 2 Comments

  • Blue Modern Advisory

    1,908 followers

    1mo Edited
    • Report this post

    Most people apply for jobs on LinkedIn without knowing what's actually happening inside the company. We built a system in Clay that does and connects you straight to the recruiter's inbox. What is Clay? A data automation tool that pulls live information from across the web. Think of it as a supercharged spreadsheet that works while you sleep. What did we build? A table that automatically tracks job postings, enriches them with company data, scores each opportunity on fit, and surfaces the recruiter's direct email. No portal. No queue. How does it work? Five steps, fully automated: 1️⃣ Filter: Clay and our model does the work to list only the companies worth targeting. 2️⃣ Flag: It tracks who you've already contacted. 3️⃣ Enrich: Provides hiring volume (eg: Microsoft has 8,400+ open roles right now). 4️⃣ Score: Auto-ranks roles by fit for your circumstances. 5️⃣ Outreach: Receive the recruiter's direct work email. Straight to the inbox. Why does this matter?  We've used this same framework at Blue Modern Advisory to move $150M in deal flow for private equity firms and generate $10M+ in pipeline for startups. Now we're using it to help students skip the application portal entirely. The process doesn't change across these contexts. Job postings are one of the most overlooked pieces of information in GTM, and what people miss is what's actually inside the job descriptions. For larger companies especially, you can dissect everything happening inside a business just from their listings like internal expansions, new initiatives and hiring velocity. Curious how to build this yourself? Drop a comment or DM us at Blue Modern Advisory! 📎 ✨

    5
